questionText: In Texas, a towing contractor orally agreed with several subcontractor towing companies that, for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract, the subcontractors would provide towing and storage services and in return would receive a share of the work or related benefits. No signed writing was ever made. The contractor later argued that the arrangement could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early. Is the agreement enforceable? Analyze.

responses.responseText:
Under Texas law, the parties’ oral agreement is generally enforceable if it satisfies basic contract requirements (agreement/meeting of the minds, definite enough terms, and consideration). The main obstacle raised by the towing contractor is the Statute of Frauds—specifically the “one-year” rule.

1) Contract requirements / consideration
Here, the oral arrangement appears to have classic consideration: the subcontractors promised to provide towing and storage during the relevant period, and in return they would receive a share of work or related benefits. If the subcontractors actually performed (or otherwise undertook the promised performance), that supports the existence of a contract and consideration.

Texas courts will enforce even an informal agreement if the material terms are reasonably certain. The prompt does not describe the “share” formula (percentage, how work is allocated, pricing/benefits details). If the “share of the work or related benefits” is too indefinite to determine what each side was owed, that could provide an additional enforcement problem. But assuming the parties’ course of dealing and the contractor’s control/selection of jobs reasonably supply the missing mechanics, the lack of detail alone usually goes to enforceability of specific damages rather than making the entire agreement void—whereas the Statute of Frauds is a categorical defense if it applies.

2) Statute of Frauds (Texas “one-year” rule)
Texas’ Statute of Frauds provides that a contract is unenforceable unless it is in writing if it is “not to be performed within one year from the date of making the agreement.” (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6).)

On the face of the facts, the parties agreed the subcontractors would participate “for the five-year term” of the contractor’s government towing contract. A fixed five-year term ordinarily means the agreement cannot be fully performed within one year, which would ordinarily trigger § 26.01(b)(6).

However, Texas courts use a key limitation: the analysis asks whether, at the time the agreement was made, it was possible to complete performance within one year—not whether it actually took longer. So the contractor’s argument that the arrangement “could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early” matters.

A. If early termination of the government contract was possible (and the subcontractors’ obligations were tied to that contingency)
If the subcontractors’ performance obligation ran only for the duration that the contractor’s government towing contract remained in effect, and that government contract could legally terminate early (for example, for convenience, change in law, cancellation by the government, loss of funding, etc.), then complete performance of the subcontractors’ side would also be possible within one year (because the underlying government contract ending would end the subcontractors’ job obligation and thus “finish” the contract). In that scenario, § 26.01(b)(6) typically does not bar enforcement, because the agreement was capable of full performance within one year.

In short: a “five-year” label does not automatically mean the oral agreement falls within the one-year Statute of Frauds if the agreement is, by its terms, contingent on an external event that could occur within a year, ending the relationship and completing performance.

B. If the government contract was effectively non-terminable (or only terminable for breach)
If, at the time of contracting, the government towing contract was not realistically terminable within one year except by breach (e.g., the term was guaranteed and cancellation could only occur upon default by the contractor), then the subcontractors’ obligations would not be capable of full performance within one year. In that scenario, § 26.01(b)(6) would likely apply and—because there is no signed writing—the oral agreement would generally be unenforceable as a contract.

3) Even if barred as a contract, recovery may still be possible
If the Statute of Frauds applies and the subcontractors cannot enforce the contract as such, Texas generally still allows recovery for value conferred under theories like quantum meruit/unjust enrichment for services actually rendered. What they usually cannot get in that circumstance is enforcement of the bargain’s full benefit (e.g., the expectation of a guaranteed five-year share of future work).

4) Likely outcome on these facts
Given the way the deal is described—subcontractors providing services “for the five-year term” of the contractor’s government towing contract—the contract is more likely to be viewed as tethered to the life of the government contract. If the government contract was terminable early in the ordinary course (i.e., not only upon breach), then the contractor’s “could have ended sooner” argument supports the position that performance was possible within one year, which generally defeats a § 26.01(b)(6) Statute of Frauds defense. That makes the agreement more likely enforceable despite the lack of a writing.

If, however, the government contract was effectively a true fixed five-year term with no early termination rights, the contractor’s no-writing defense is much stronger, and the agreement would likely be unenforceable as a contract under § 26.01(b)(6).

Bottom line: The agreement is most likely enforceable if early termination of the underlying government contract was possible at the time the oral deal was made (so the parties’ performances could have been fully completed within one year). If early termination was not possible absent breach, then the oral “five-year” agreement likely falls within Texas’ one-year Statute of Frauds and would be unenforceable as a contract (though subcontractors may still pursue payment for services actually provided).

Under Texas law, an oral contract is generally enforceable unless a specific statute (most commonly the Texas statute of frauds) requires a writing.

1) Existence and basic enforceability of the oral deal
You have the usual elements:
- Offer/acceptance: the contractor orally agreed that subcontractors would provide towing and storage during the relevant period.
- Consideration: subcontractors provided services; in return they received a “share of the work or related benefits.”
- Mutual intent and performance: the arrangement is described as a bargained-for, five-year-term structure tied to the contractor’s government towing contract.

So the agreement is not automatically unenforceable just because it was oral, and Texas recognizes contracts for services without a writing if no statute of frauds applies.

2) The key issue: Texas statute of frauds (one-year rule)
Texas has a statute of frauds covering agreements “that are not to be performed within one year from the making of the agreement” unless there is a signed writing. In practical terms, this is the “one-year” test.

The relevant point is that Texas looks at what was possible at the time the parties made the agreement—not what happened later. If full performance could have been completed within one year, then the statute of frauds usually does not apply even if the contract actually lasted longer. Conversely, if the agreement required performance for a fixed period longer than one year and could not be completed within one year, then it falls within the statute of frauds and is generally unenforceable without a writing.

Application to these facts:
- The arrangement was for the “five-year term” of the contractor’s government towing contract.
- However, the contractor argues the arrangement “could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early.”

That argument matters because if the subcontractor’s obligations were truly coterminous with (or otherwise dependent on) the contractor’s government contract, then early termination of the government program would end the performance and make completion possible within one year (at least in some scenarios). Under the “possible within one year” framework, that can take the agreement outside the statute of frauds even though the contemplated duration was five years.

On the other hand, if the subcontract agreement was structured so that the subcontractors were still obligated to perform for the full five-year period (or the contractor still owed the subcontractors their share/benefits) regardless of whether the government program ended early, then full performance would not be capable of occurring within one year. In that version of the deal, the one-year statute of frauds would likely apply and the lack of a writing would make enforcement difficult or barred.

3) Likely conclusion on enforceability (based on the facts as stated)
Given the prompt’s description—“for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract” and the contractor’s asserted possibility of early program termination—there is a strong argument that the subcontractors’ performance period was contingent on the government contract’s continued existence. If that is correct, then it was possible, from the outset, for the agreement to be fully performed within one year due to early termination by the government. Under Texas’s “possibility” approach, that typically means the statute of frauds would not render the oral agreement unenforceable.

So, the agreement is likely enforceable, assuming:
- the subcontractors’ duties (and the contractor’s reciprocal duties) genuinely ended if the government program terminated, and
- the agreement’s material terms (especially what triggers the “share of the work or related benefits” and how it is calculated) can be proven with enough definiteness to enforce.

4) Additional enforceability caveat: indefiniteness of the “share”
Even if the statute of frauds does not bar enforcement, Texas courts still require enough certainty to determine the parties’ obligations. A “share of the work or related benefits” can be enforceable if there is an objective way to determine what the “share” means (for example, a percentage of dispatched tows, a defined allocation method, or a formula tied to measurable program activity). If the “share” is too vague to determine without speculation, the court could limit or refuse relief.

Final answer
Most likely, yes—the oral agreement is enforceable in Texas because, if the subcontractors’ obligations were tied to and would have ended with early termination of the government program, then the contract was capable of being fully performed within one year and thus would not fall within the one-year statute of frauds. Enforcement would still depend on proving the agreement’s material terms (especially the basis for the “share of the work/benefits”) with sufficient definiteness.

Under Texas law, an oral agreement is generally enforceable unless a statute of frauds requirement applies or the agreement is too indefinite to enforce.

1) Statute of frauds (“one-year rule”)
Texas requires a writing for certain agreements, including agreements “that are not to be performed within one year from the making of the agreement.” Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6).

At first glance, an oral agreement “for the five-year term” looks like it would not be fully performed within one year, so the statute-of-frauds defense is a natural one.

However, Texas applies the “incapable of performance within one year” test: the statute applies only if, by the agreement’s terms at the time it is made, the contract cannot possibly be fully performed within one year. If full performance could occur within one year due to a contingency that the agreement itself contemplates, the statute of frauds usually does not bar enforcement.

Here, the bargain was expressly tied to “the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract.” If the government towing contract could be terminated early (and the subcontractors’ duties were coextensive with that government contract), then the entire arrangement could end—meaning it could be fully performed—within a year if the government terminated early. That aligns with the contractor’s argument: the agreement was not necessarily “incapable” of ending (and therefore being fully performed) within one year.

So, if the oral agreement’s operative duration was effectively “during the life of the government towing contract (as it may terminate early),” then the one-year statute-of-frauds bar is likely not triggered.

If, by contrast, the oral agreement was a fixed promise to provide services and/or receive benefits for the entire five-year period regardless of early termination of the government contract (i.e., no contingency ending performance), then it is much more likely the agreement is within § 26.01(b)(6) and therefore unenforceable absent a writing or a separate exception.

2) Definiteness and consideration
Even if the statute of frauds does not apply, the subcontractors still must show an enforceable contract—i.e., mutual assent and sufficiently definite terms.

The services aspect (towing and storage) sounds definite enough. The payment/benefit side is described as “a share of the work or related benefits.” Whether that is enforceable depends on whether there was a workable, objective way to determine what “share” meant (for example: a defined percentage, a defined pricing/crediting method, or a clear allocation process). If the “share” was left entirely to the contractor’s discretion with no standard, Texas courts may find the agreement too indefinite to enforce as a contract for damages.

That said, Texas courts commonly allow at least restitution/quantum meruit recovery for services actually performed and accepted, even when the overall contract is unenforceable or the compensation terms are unclear.

3) Likely result on your facts
On the specific question you gave—whether the agreement is enforceable and how the contractor’s “it could have ended early” argument affects enforceability—the strongest statutory analysis is:

- If the parties’ intent was that the subcontractor relationship ran only so long as the contractor’s government towing contract ran (so early government termination would end the arrangement), then the agreement is likely not barred by the statute of frauds because it was possible to be fully performed within one year.
- If the parties intended an absolute five-year commitment independent of early government termination, then the oral agreement is likely barred by § 26.01(b)(6).

Even if not barred by the statute of frauds, enforceability will still hinge on whether the “share of the work/benefits” terms were sufficiently definite to be enforceable as a contract; otherwise, the subcontractors may still have a path to recover the value of services actually provided.

If you want, tell me what evidence exists about (a) whether the subcontractors’ obligations automatically ended when the government contract ended, and (b) how the “share” was calculated in practice—those details usually determine the final outcome.

Under Texas law, the main obstacle to enforcing an oral contract with a multi-year duration is the statute of frauds. A Texas oral agreement is generally unenforceable if, “by its terms,” it cannot be performed within one year of the date it is made. Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6).

1) Does the five-year term make the oral agreement unenforceable under the statute of frauds?
The agreement here was “for the five-year term of” the contractor’s government towing contract. On its face, “five years” suggests performance extends beyond one year.

However, the statute of frauds turns on whether the contract is capable of being fully performed within one year—not on whether it is likely to take longer. Texas courts apply a “capable of performance” test: if the agreement, by its terms, could be completed within one year, then it is not within § 26.01(b)(6), even if it actually ends up lasting longer.

The contractor’s own argument—that the arrangement “could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early”—matters. If the parties’ understanding was that the subcontractors’ obligation to provide towing/storage (and the contractor’s obligation to share benefits/work with them) would track the life of the government contract, then an early termination of the government contract would end the subcontract relationship. In that scenario, the agreement is not categorically incapable of full performance within one year, because full performance could occur if the government contract ended within the first year.

So, if the oral deal was effectively “for so long as the government towing contract runs,” and it could end early on government termination, the better view is that § 26.01(b)(6) does not bar enforcement.

2) What if the agreement is construed as a fixed five-year commitment regardless of early termination?
The analysis changes if a court finds the parties intended an unconditional five-year obligation that survives early government termination. For example, if the subcontractors were promised work/benefits for five years even if the government contract ended early, then the agreement would likely be “not performable within one year” by its terms, and the lack of a written contract would make it unenforceable under § 26.01(b)(6).

But your stated facts point the other way: the term was explicitly tied to the government towing contract’s five-year term, and the contractor acknowledges it could end sooner if the government program ended early. That linkage supports the “could be completed within one year” argument.

3) Even if the contract were barred by the statute of frauds, the subcontractors may still recover in equity
If a court nevertheless holds the oral agreement is barred by the statute of frauds, that typically prevents enforcement of the contract itself (i.e., it blocks contract damages based on the oral terms). It does not necessarily prevent the subcontractors from seeking restitution for the value of services actually provided and accepted (e.g., quantum meruit / unjust enrichment), depending on the proof and pleadings.

4) Overall conclusion
Most likely, the agreement is enforceable because the contract was tied to the contractor’s government towing contract and could end early if the government program terminated; that makes it capable of full performance within one year, so the statute of frauds under Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6) likely does not apply.

If, contrary to that linkage, the oral agreement is treated as an unconditional five-year commitment independent of early termination, then it would likely be unenforceable for lack of a writing. In either case, if the subcontractors actually performed, they may still have alternative restitution-based remedies even if the oral contract is not enforceable.

If you want, I can also analyze (separately) whether the “share of the work or related benefits” term is sufficiently definite to enforce as a contract under Texas contract-formation requirements, because definiteness can be the next major issue once the statute-of-frauds problem is resolved.

Under Texas law, an oral agreement is generally enforceable unless a statute (most commonly the statute of frauds) bars it. Here the key issue is the Texas “one-year” statute of frauds.

1) Contract formation and definiteness
A court would first ask whether there was an actual agreement with sufficiently definite terms. From your facts: (a) the contractor and multiple towing subcontractors orally agreed the subcontractors would provide towing and storage services, and (b) in return the subcontractors would receive a “share of the work or related benefits” for a defined term (the term of the contractor’s government towing contract). That is classic “services for compensation” consideration, so the arrangement is the kind of thing Texas will recognize as a contract if the parties’ understandings are provable (for example, the method of calculating the “share,” or at least enough to determine damages). Any uncertainty about exactly how the “share” is computed goes more to damages and proof than to outright existence of the contract, unless it is so indefinite that a factfinder cannot determine what was promised.

2) The one-year statute of frauds (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6))
Texas generally requires a written memorandum for “a contract that is not to be performed within one year from the date of making.” The question is not whether performance would likely take longer than a year, but whether performance is impossible within one year from the making of the contract.

A) The contractor’s argument points toward the statute of frauds—but it’s not automatic
The contractor’s oral agreement was described as covering the “five-year term” of the government towing contract. Standing alone, an agreement that by its terms commits both sides to a fixed five-year performance period is typically within the one-year statute of frauds because it cannot be fully performed within one year.

B) But the “could have ended sooner” argument matters under Texas’s impossibility test
You specifically state the contractor argues the arrangement “could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early.”

That matters because, if the parties’ agreement was coextensive with the government contract—i.e., the subcontractors’ performance obligation (and the contractor’s obligation to give the “share”) ended when the government program ended—then the contract could be fully performed within one year if the government terminated within the first year. Under Texas law’s “impossible to perform” framing, the mere fact that the government contract was initially expected to last five years does not automatically trigger the statute of frauds if early termination made completion within one year legally possible.

So, if the oral deal was truly: “You will provide services and receive a share for as long as the government contract runs; if the government contract ends early, our deal ends,” then it is plausible a court would find the oral agreement is not barred by § 26.01(b)(6).

C) When the statute of frauds would likely apply anyway
If, instead, the parties’ oral agreement required the subcontractors to provide towing/storage for a fixed five-year period regardless of whether the government program ended (or required the contractor to pay the “share of work/benefits” for five years even if there was no government program), then performance would be impossible within one year and the statute of frauds would likely bar enforcement absent a writing.

On your facts as stated (“for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract”), the more natural reading is that the five-year duration was tied to the government contract’s duration rather than an absolute five-year commitment independent of it. That usually favors enforceability (or at least favors arguing the statute of frauds does not apply).

3) If barred by the statute of frauds, do any exceptions rescue enforcement?
With no signed writing, enforcement would have to rely on something like an equitable estoppel theory or another doctrine. Texas courts are sometimes reluctant to use equitable theories to override the statute of frauds, but they can in limited circumstances where one party reasonably relied on the promise to their detriment. Those are fact-intensive and not guaranteed. Your prompt doesn’t provide details on reliance, inducement, or whether the subcontractors performed in expectation of guaranteed “share” benefits beyond what they actually received, so the safest conclusion is that the only clearly “on point” issue you’ve given is the one-year statute of frauds analysis, not an established exception.

4) Bottom-line conclusion
- If the oral agreement was coextensive with the government towing contract—so that early termination of the government program would also end (or at least allow completion of) the subcontractors’ obligations within one year—then the agreement is likely enforceable because performance within one year was legally possible, so § 26.01(b)(6) would not bar it.
- If the oral agreement required the subcontractors/contractor to continue for a fixed five-year period regardless of early termination (i.e., not realistically tied to the government contract’s actual end date), then it would likely fall within the one-year statute of frauds and be unenforceable without a writing.

Given your phrasing (“for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract”) and the contractor’s own argument that it could end early, the more likely legal result is that the oral agreement would not be barred by Texas’s one-year statute of frauds—so it is likely enforceable, assuming the parties can prove the essential terms well enough to establish what “share of the work or related benefits” was promised and what performance occurred.

In Texas, the key issue with an oral contract for a multi-year arrangement is usually the Texas statute of frauds—specifically the “one-year” provision.

1) Statute of frauds (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6))
Texas generally requires a signed writing for an agreement “that is not to be performed within one year from the date of the making of the agreement.”

A five-year term, standing alone, suggests the contract cannot be completed within one year, and would ordinarily fall within the statute of frauds.

However, Texas applies an objective “could it be fully performed within one year?” test. If, by the contract’s terms, it is possible (even if unlikely) that the parties’ obligations could be completely performed within one year, the statute of frauds does not apply, and the oral contract can be enforceable.

Here, the oral arrangement was: the subcontractors would provide towing and storage “for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract.” If the government towing contract could be terminated early, then the subcontractor arrangement would likewise end earlier, meaning the agreement could be fully performed within one year if early termination occurred within that first year.

That is exactly the logic the contractor is arguing (“it could have ended sooner if the government program was terminated early”). Under the Texas one-year statute-of-frauds framework, that argument is strong, because it points to an objective possibility of completion within one year based on the contingency tied to the government contract.

So, assuming the parties’ obligations truly were tied to the government contract’s term (i.e., the subcontractors’ work obligation and the contractor’s obligation to share benefits likewise ended when the government towing contract ended), the oral agreement is likely outside § 26.01(b)(6) and therefore not barred merely because it was for “five years.”

2) If the five-year term was unconditional, the result could change
If the oral agreement was effectively fixed for five years regardless of early termination of the government contract—i.e., the subcontractors were still obligated (and/or the contractor was still obligated to ensure the benefit-sharing) for the full five-year period even if the government program ended—then the contract would not be capable of full performance within one year “by its terms,” and the statute of frauds would likely render it unenforceable absent a writing.

The facts you provided lean toward the contingent interpretation (“five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract”), but the enforceability turns on that actual structure.

3) Other enforceability requirements (mutual assent and sufficiently definite terms)
Even if the statute of frauds does not apply, the subcontractors still must show a contract: mutual assent and consideration, plus terms definite enough to enforce. “A share of the work or related benefits” can be enforceable if the parties had an understood method for calculating the share (percentage, fee component, or other determinable measure) and the parties performed consistently with that understanding. If the “share” was too indefinite (no agreed basis for what the subcontractors get), a court may decline to enforce as a contract, though it might still allow recovery on equitable theories (like quantum meruit) depending on the proof—those are separate from “enforcement of the contract itself.”

4) Practical conclusion
Based on the one-year statute of frauds analysis, an oral agreement is likely enforceable in this scenario because the obligations were tied to a government towing contract whose early termination made it objectively possible for the arrangement to end (and therefore be fully performed) within one year.

Counterfactually, if the five-year obligation was unconditional notwithstanding early termination of the government program, the agreement would more likely be barred by § 26.01(b)(6) and thus unenforceable absent a signed writing.

- The arrangement is described as lasting “for the five-year term” of the contractor’s government towing contract.

How the contractor’s “could have ended sooner” argument matters
- If the government towing program/contract could terminate early, and that early termination would end the parties’ obligations under the subcontract arrangement, then complete performance (i.e., the work relationship running its course) could occur within one year.
- Under Texas’s “capable of performance” approach, that contingency can take the oral agreement outside the one-year statute of frauds, even though the contract is generally contemplated to run five years.

Practical application to these facts
- If the oral subcontract agreement was truly tied to the contractor’s government contract and had no independent obligation guaranteeing five years of towing/storage services regardless of government termination, then early termination is a contingency under which the subcontract obligations could end within one year. In that scenario, the oral agreement is more likely enforceable despite lacking a writing.
- If, instead, the subcontractors were obligated (and the contractor promised) that the arrangement would continue for the full five-year term even if the government program ended early—i.e., the five-year duration was not actually contingent—then it would likely fall squarely within § 26.01(b)(6), and without a signed writing the agreement would generally be unenforceable.

2) Meeting-of-the-terms / definiteness (secondary enforceability issue)
Even if the statute of frauds does not bar enforcement, Texas courts still require that the contract be sufficiently definite to show (among other things) mutual assent and the essential terms of consideration. Here, “in return would receive a share of the work or related benefits” can be enforceable if:
- there is a workable method for determining what the subcontractors would receive (e.g., assignment of towing jobs from the contractor to the subcontractors, or a definable measure of “share”), and
- the contractor’s promise is not merely illusory (e.g., not simply an unrestricted right to give them nothing).

But if “share of work or related benefits” is too vague, or if the contractor had absolute discretion to provide no work while still claiming the subcontractors were bound for five years, the agreement could be attacked as unenforceable for indefiniteness or as lacking real mutual obligations.

3) Likely bottom line on the specific question asked
On the question you posed—whether the agreement is enforceable given the contractor’s argument that it “could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early”—the strongest legal answer is:

- The agreement is likely enforceable against a statute-of-frauds challenge if the parties’ obligations were contingent on the continued existence of the government towing program/contract such that the subcontract could be fully performed (and then terminate) within one year upon early government termination. In that case, § 26.01(b)(6) would not require a writing because the contract was capable of being performed within one year.

- The agreement would be at risk of being unenforceable for lack of a writing only if the “five-year term” was treated as a fixed obligation not subject to early termination affecting the subcontractor obligations, meaning performance could not be completed within one year under the parties’ agreement as made.

Under Texas law, an oral agreement is generally enforceable unless a statute of frauds provision applies. The most likely issue here is Texas’s “one-year” statute of frauds.

1) Does the oral subcontract arrangement fall within the one-year statute of frauds?
Texas has a statute of frauds requiring a signed writing for certain agreements, including agreements “that are not to be performed within one year from the date of making.” Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6).

If the oral deal is understood as requiring the subcontractors to provide towing and storage services for a fixed five-year period, then—viewed at the time it was made—full performance would not occur within one year. In that scenario, the agreement would ordinarily be within § 26.01(b)(6) and unenforceable unless there is a writing.

However, the key Texas rule is that the relevant question is whether the contract, by its terms, is capable of being fully performed within one year—not whether that outcome is likely.

2) Does the contractor’s “it could have ended early” argument defeat the statute of frauds defense?
The contractor’s argument goes to exactly that “capable of performance within one year” concept: the government towing program (and thus the contractor’s government towing contract) could have been terminated early. If the subcontract arrangement was truly coterminous with the government contract—i.e., the parties’ obligation to provide services (and the contractor’s obligation to give the subcontractors their share of work/benefits) automatically ended when the government program ended—then there is a plausible reading that the oral agreement could have been fully performed within one year if the government contract terminated early (for example, within the first year). In that case, § 26.01(b)(6) would not bar enforcement because the contract would be capable of completion within one year.

On the other hand, if the subcontractors’ obligations were for a fixed five-year term regardless of whether the government contract ended early (for example, the subcontractors were still required to stand ready or provide services for the full five years even if the government program ended), then early termination would be irrelevant to “capable of performance,” and the statute of frauds would likely apply.

Based on your facts (“for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract”), the strongest interpretation is that the subcontract term ran with the government contract term. That tends to support the contractor’s position that the arrangement could have ended early, which in turn tends to undercut application of § 26.01(b)(6). The statute of frauds is also an affirmative defense, so the contractor would generally have to show the writing requirement applies; conversely, if the subcontractors can show the agreement was contingent/coterminous with a government contract that could end within a year, that is typically what prevents the statute of frauds from defeating the oral deal.

3) Even if the statute of frauds does not bar enforcement, can the agreement be too indefinite?
Separate from the statute of frauds, Texas law requires enforceable contracts to have reasonably definite material terms. Here, the compensation concept is described as “a share of the work or related benefits.” If the parties never agreed on essential details (for example, the percentage split, how “share” is calculated, what determines the pool of work/benefits, billing responsibility, when payment is due, etc.), the agreement could be challenged as too indefinite.

Texas courts apply the statute of frauds strictly and do not recognize broad exceptions beyond those explicitly codified. **Partial Performance**: Some jurisdictions allow partial performance to take an oral contract out of the statute of frauds, particularly for land sales. **Estoppel**: Texas recognizes promissory estoppel as a potential defense to the statute of frauds, but only in narrow circumstances where the party seeking enforcement has relied on the promise to their detriment in a way that makes enforcement necessary to prevent fraud or injustice. # Enforceability Analysis of the Oral Towing Services Agreement

## Statute of Frauds Issue

The primary enforceability problem is the Texas statute of frauds. Under Texas Business & Commerce Code § 2.201, contracts for the sale of goods must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. However, this agreement involves services (towing and storage), not the sale of goods, so the UCC statute of frauds does not directly apply.

Texas common law recognizes a statute of frauds for certain categories of contracts, including those that cannot be performed within one year. See Tex. Bus. & Commerce Code § 26.01 (which applies the one-year rule to contracts generally under the common law).

## The One-Year Rule Application

The critical issue is whether this agreement falls within the one-year statute of frauds. The agreement explicitly states a five-year term. Under Texas law, a contract that by its terms cannot be performed within one year must be in writing to be enforceable.

However, Texas courts have applied an important exception: if the contract could possibly be performed within one year, even if performance within that timeframe is unlikely or improbable, the statute of frauds does not apply. The question is whether the contract could be performed, not whether it would be.

The contractor's argument that the arrangement "could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early" is significant. If the government contract could be terminated within one year, then this subcontracting arrangement could theoretically be performed (and terminated) within one year. Under Texas's flexible approach to the one-year rule, this possibility of early termination might take the agreement outside the statute of frauds, making it enforceable despite the lack of a writing.

## Partial Performance Doctrine

Even if the statute of frauds technically applies, Texas recognizes the partial performance exception. If the subcontractors have begun performance by actually providing towing and storage services, this part performance might remove the agreement from the statute of frauds. However, part performance alone is typically insufficient in Texas; the performance must be of such a nature that it cannot be explained except by reference to the alleged oral contract.

## Conclusion

The agreement is likely **enforceable** despite the lack of a writing, primarily because:

1. The possibility of early termination of the government contract means the arrangement could theoretically be completed within one year, placing it outside the strict application of the one-year statute of frauds rule under Texas law.

2. If the subcontractors have performed services, partial performance may also support enforceability.

However, the enforceability depends heavily on evidence that early termination was genuinely possible and on the extent of any performance by the subcontractors. The contractor's own argument about possible early termination actually weakens its position by suggesting the contract was not necessarily a five-year obligation.

Under Texas law, an oral contract is generally enforceable unless (1) the parties’ agreement falls within the Statute of Frauds or (2) the agreement is too indefinite to enforce.

1) Statute of Frauds (Texas “one-year” rule)
Texas Business & Commerce Code § 26.01(b)(6) provides that a promise that “is not to be performed within one year from the date of making the agreement” is unenforceable unless there is a sufficient writing signed by the party to be charged.

Here, the oral arrangement contemplated that the subcontractors would provide towing and storage “for the five-year term” of the contractor’s government towing contract. On its face, a fixed five-year contemplated duration often triggers the one-year Statute of Frauds.

However, Texas courts apply a “possibility” test: the key question is whether full performance of the agreement was possible within one year at the time the oral contract was made—not whether it was likely. If the contract by its terms could be fully performed within one year, the Statute of Frauds does not apply, even if the agreement was expected to last longer.

The contractor’s argument that the arrangement “could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early” points directly to that possibility. If, as the facts suggest, the subcontractors’ obligation (and the contractor’s obligation to keep receiving towing/storage from them under the profit/benefit-sharing arrangement) was tied to the life of the government towing contract—i.e., it would end automatically when that government contract ended—then full performance could occur within one year if the government contract were terminated within the first year.

In that scenario, the Statute of Frauds likely does not bar enforcement because performance within one year was possible at the time of contracting.

Important caveat: if the oral arrangement instead obligated the subcontractors to provide services for a guaranteed five-year commitment regardless of early termination of the government contract (for example, the contractor still had to ensure a five-year benefit/payment stream), then it would be substantially harder to argue the agreement could be “performed within one year” as a matter of contract terms. But based on your description (“for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract”), the more natural reading is that the subcontractors’ work term tracked the government contract’s duration, making early termination a realistic endpoint.

2) Definiteness and proof of the oral terms
Even if the Statute of Frauds does not apply, Texas requires enough definiteness to show (a) the parties intended to make a contract, (b) there was mutual assent, and (c) the essential terms are ascertainable.

The payment/consideration here is described as a “share of the work or related benefits.” That can be enforceable in Texas (profit/commission-like structures are not automatically too indefinite), but the subcontractors will still need evidence of how the “share” was determined or applied (e.g., allocation method, expected percentages, or how work was distributed in practice). If the arrangement was completely open-ended—no method at all for calculating the share, no identifiable standard for what constituted the “benefits,” and no consistent course of performance—then a court could find the compensation term too indefinite to enforce.

That said, the fact that the subcontractors actually provided towing/storage services under the arrangement often helps: Texas courts commonly treat a consistent course of performance and contemporaneous documents (texts/emails, invoices, work orders, billing practices) as evidence of what the parties meant by the “share” and scope.

3) Bottom line
Most likely, the agreement is enforceable despite being oral, because:
- the “five-year” reference is tied to the government contract’s duration, and
- early termination of the government contract means the agreement could have been fully performed within one year (a recognized way to avoid the one-year Statute of Frauds under Texas law).

That said, enforceability still depends on the subcontractors proving the essential terms with reasonable certainty—especially how the “share of the work or related benefits” worked in practice—and showing the parties intended to be bound.
